---
title: "Why we default to cell-means coding (`0 + G`) in the direct 4PL"
date: 2026-06-26
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
---

## The question

Throughout the supplement the direct fits are written with the intercept
suppressed — `ctmax = ~ 0 + species`, `z = ~ 0 + oxygen`,
`ctmax = ~ 0 + recovery + (1 | plant)`, etc. Do we *need* to suppress the
intercept? Shouldn't `tls()` recover any per-group quantity regardless of how
the linear predictor is coded?

**Short answer:** we do not need it for identifiability or recovery — the user's
instinct is correct. We default to it for *prior symmetry* and readability.

## Cell-means vs treatment coding are the same model

`~ 0 + G` (cell-means / one-hot) and `~ G` (treatment / reference) are two
parameterisations of the *same* linear predictor. Both design matrices span the
identical column space — the space of per-group means. Therefore:

- the likelihood is identical,
- the fitted values are identical,
- `tls(by = "G")` recovers every per-group $CT_{max}$, $z$, $T_{crit}$ **and**
  any between-group contrast from either coding.

With flat / non-informative priors the two codings give bit-identical
posteriors. Suppressing the intercept buys nothing at the likelihood level. The
4PL is fully identified from the dose–response data either way.

## Why we still default to `0 + G`: the priors are load-bearing

The package does **not** use flat priors. `make_4pl_priors()`
(`R/priors.R`) ships weakly-informative, coding-aware priors. A flat prior on
the asymptotes produced 191 divergences (see
`notes/2026-06-21-prior-sensitivity-direct-ctmax-z.qmd`), so the centred priors
are doing real work.

The relevant direct-mode priors:

| sub-parameter | centre   | level SD | slope/contrast SD |
|---------------|----------|----------|-------------------|
| `CTmaxdev`    | 0        | 10       | 10                |
| `logz`        | `log(3)` | 0.7      | 0.7               |

`direct_par()` places the **centred** prior either on each factor *level*
(cell-means) or on the *Intercept* (treatment), and a separate **mean-zero**
prior on everything else (temperature slopes AND between-group contrasts).

- Under **cell-means** (`0 + G`): every coefficient is an *absolute* group-level
  parameter, so the same biologically-meaningful prior lands identically on
  every group. Groups are symmetric a priori.
- Under **treatment** coding: the intercept is the reference group's absolute
  parameter; the rest are *contrasts*. The contrast prior is deliberately
  centred on **zero** (not on `log(3)`), which keeps the per-group prior **mean**
  coding-invariant and removes a `log(3)` shrinkage bias (otherwise the model is
  told the between-group z-ratio "should" be ~3×).

So treatment coding is not *wrong* — the package is built so it recovers
approximately the same per-group answers. Cell-means is just the cleaner home
for the priors.

## The one cost of treatment coding: prior variance is not coding-invariant

The prior **mean** is coding-invariant by construction; the prior **variance**
is not. Under treatment coding a non-reference group's implied prior convolves
the intercept SD and the contrast SD. For `logz`:

$$
\mathrm{SD}_{\text{non-ref}} = \sqrt{0.7^2 + 0.7^2} \approx 0.99
\quad\text{vs}\quad 0.70 \ \text{under cell-means.}
$$

So treatment coding silently gives the reference group a tighter prior than the
others — the groups are not symmetric a priori. And `normalize_cellmeans()` only
rewrites single-factor formulas, so multi-factor models can't be auto-fixed.
This is exactly why the docstring recommends `0 + G` for multi-group models.

## Bottom line

- **Needed for recovery?** No. Identifiability does not require it.
- **Why default to it?** Symmetric, identical weakly-informative priors across
  groups, plus `summary()` reads per-group directly (each group's $CT_{max}$/$z$
  rather than reference + offsets).
- With data-dominated fits (aphids, zebrafish) the posterior is essentially the
  same either way.

## Supplement wording flag

`ms/supplement.qmd` (~line 1695) currently states cell-means "returns the
**same** per-group estimates as treatment coding." That is exact only for the
prior *mean*; the prior *variance* differs (the convolution above). For these
fits the difference is immaterial, but the strictly defensible wording is
"essentially the same per-group **point** estimates."

## See also

- `R/priors.R` — `make_4pl_priors()`, `direct_par()`, `cell_means_coefs()`.
- `notes/2026-06-21-prior-sensitivity-direct-ctmax-z.qmd` — divergence /
  prior-sensitivity evidence for the centred priors.
